us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With War, Declines to Rule Out Ground Troops
us-politics.body:
Trump posted an Easter warning threatening Iran with 'Hell' if it blocks the Strait of Hormuz, and declined to rule out ground troops if nuclear talks collapse. The threat came days after a US service member went missing during operations in Iran, with Tehran claiming American aircraft were destroyed in the recovery mission. Netanyahu congratulated Trump on rescuing a downed pilot, signaling close coordination.

Opinion is fractured across party lines - and within them. House Republicans say any ground deployment should target enriched uranium, not territory. Democrats warn a war would be strategically costly and fiscally reckless. A former Trump adviser said the aggressive rhetoric makes a deal less likely, not more. Even Marjorie Taylor Greene broke with Trump, calling the Easter threat 'insane.' NATO allies are watching uneasily as Trump's pressure campaign escalates.

sources.title: Netanyahu says he spoke with Trump, congratulated him on rescuing downed pilot
Iran says US aircraft destroyed during recovery mission for missing service member
House Republican says he doesn’t think ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz
Marjorie Taylor Greene rails against ‘insane’ Trump after Easter Iran threat
Top Armed Services Democrat says Iran war ‘mistake in large part because of the cost’
Trump declines to rule out ground troops if Iran doesn’t make a deal
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
Trump roils NATO as pressure builds over Strait of Hormuz
The war in Iran is showing why the US needs diverse energy resources
House Republican: ‘Only purpose’ for US boots on the ground in Iran is ‘to get the enriched uranium’
Former Trump adviser on president’s latest Iran threat: ‘Not going to get a deal’
House Democrat: Iran’s control of Strait of Hormuz ‘more strategically vital’ than nuclear weapon

us-politics.headline: DHS Shutdown Drags On as Republican Divisions Stall Fix
us-politics.body:
The Department of Homeland Security remains unfunded after Republican infighting and Trump's disengagement blocked a deal before the House recess. Speaker Johnson and Senate Majority Leader Thune have struggled to align their caucuses, while Trump has offered little public pressure to resolve it. House Minority Leader Jeffries called on Johnson to cancel the recess and return members to Washington.

The standoff reflects a broader tension inside the GOP between fiscal hardliners and members wary of the political cost of a security funding lapse. Democrats are framing the shutdown as a Republican governance failure. No resolution appears imminent.

sources.title: Republican divisions, Trump’s detachment stymie GOP efforts to reopen DHS
Jeffries calls on Johnson to end House recess amid DHS funding lapse

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi, Says No Further Cabinet Shake-Up Coming
us-politics.body:
Trump dismissed Attorney General Pam Bondi and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, then told reporters not to expect further Cabinet changes. He offered no detailed explanation for either firing. SNL mocked Bondi's departure in its cold open, reflecting the story's cultural reach beyond Washington.

Senate Democrats moved quickly to frame the firings as a warning. Senator Tim Kaine said Bondi's exit should be a 'lesson' for whoever is nominated next - suggesting the role carries real political risk under Trump. The back-to-back dismissals have renewed questions about loyalty tests inside the administration, though Trump himself pushed back on that framing.

sources.title: SNL rips Bondi’s departure in cold open
Trump says to not expect Cabinet shake-up after Bondi, Noem firings
Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee

us-politics.headline: Democrats Eye 2028 as Party Debates Outreach and Affordability
us-politics.body:
Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego said he is open to a 2028 presidential run, adding to a growing list of Democrats positioning early. Representative Ro Khanna argued the party must engage unconventional media figures like streamer Hasan Piker or risk losing younger voters in future elections. Stacey Abrams, meanwhile, called Trump's executive order restricting mail-in voting 'patently illegal' and signaled legal challenges ahead.

A Hill opinion piece warned that Democrats' focus on 'affordability' as a campaign message could backfire if voters tie high costs to Democratic governance rather than Republican policy. The party faces a dual challenge - rebuilding its coalition while defining a clear economic contrast with Trump heading into the midterms.

sources.title: Stacey Abrams on Trump’s mail-in voting order: ‘Patently illegal’
Khanna: Those saying ‘don’t engage’ with personalities like Hasan Piker ‘will cost us future elections’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’
‘Affordability’ could eventually bite Democrats in the bum

au-politics.headline: Fuel Tax Cuts Slash Petrol Prices as Cyclone Maila Looms
au-politics.body:
Petrol prices have fallen nearly 32 cents in a week following the government's fuel excise cuts, offering relief to households squeezed by cost-of-living pressures. The drop is already prompting some commuters to reconsider their transport choices, though e-bike retailers report a surge in sales suggesting the shift away from cars may outlast any temporary price relief.

Meanwhile, the Bureau of Meteorology is tracking Tropical Cyclone Maila as it approaches the Queensland coast, bringing scattered rain and strong winds to the east coast. Forecasters are watching the system closely as it intensifies. The dual pressures of economic relief and a looming weather emergency are shaping the week's agenda heading into the Easter long weekend.

sources.title: Australia news live: Petrol prices drop nearly 32 cents in a week after fuel tax cuts; BoM says Cyclone Maila may approach Queensland
Commuters are switching from cars to e-bikes to save money on fuel
Scattered rain, strong wind along Qld coast as forecasters watch TC Maila

au-politics.headline: Moira Deeming Wins Preselection; Liberals Divided on Censorship
au-politics.body:
Moira Deeming has secured Victorian Liberal preselection, ending a bitter internal battle that exposed deep fault lines within the state party. Her return is a rebuke to the moderate wing that sought to sideline her, and signals that conservative voices retain significant influence over candidate selection.

Separately, the Spectator has published commentary arguing that Liberal support for social media censorship laws is politically self-defeating, framing it as a betrayal of free-speech principles that traditionally anchor the party's base. The tension between those two positions - one rewarding a culture-war conservative, the other warning against regulatory overreach - reflects a broader identity struggle inside the Liberal Party ahead of the next federal cycle.

sources.title: Moira Deeming wins Victorian Liberal preselection battle
Liberals are fools to support social media censorship

au-politics.headline: ABC Privatisation Debate Resurfaces; Human Rights Complaint Targets NSW
au-politics.body:
The Spectator has revived the question of ABC privatisation, publishing a valuation piece that frames the public broadcaster as an asset ripe for sale. The ABC has long been a flashpoint between right-leaning critics who argue it carries institutional bias and defenders who see it as essential public infrastructure. No government has moved to privatise it, but the debate resurfaces reliably before elections.

On a separate front, the Australian Human Rights Commission is investigating a racial discrimination complaint against the NSW government. Details of the complaint remain limited, but the investigation adds pressure to a state government already managing multiple policy controversies. Both stories touch on contested questions about the role of public institutions and who they serve.

sources.title: Should the ABC be privatised? A modest valuation…
Australian Human Rights Commission investigates racial discrimination complaint against NSW government

au-politics.headline: Youth Cells Condemned; Zone RV Collapse Sparks ASIC Push
au-politics.body:
A government watchdog has called for urgent upgrades to youth detention cells it describes as harmful, warning that current conditions obstruct access to justice for young people in custody. The finding puts pressure on state authorities to act, though no timeline for reform has been set.

In a separate consumer protection story, customers who lost a combined $10 million in the collapse of caravan manufacturer Zone RV are demanding an ASIC investigation. The company's failure left buyers without vehicles they had paid for in full. Both cases point to gaps in oversight - one in the justice system, one in corporate regulation - that advocates say require stronger government intervention.

sources.title: Watchdog seeks upgrade to 'harmful' youth cells to improve access to justice
Angry customers who lost $10m in Zone RV collapse urge ASIC to investigate

world.headline: Iran War Escalates as Hormuz Standoff Reaches Breaking Point
world.body:
Tehran has rejected Trump's Tuesday deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, defying an expletive-laden social media threat from the president that warned of severe consequences. A US airman was rescued from a remote part of Iran in an operation Trump declared a victory, though military officials warn threats to US forces in the region remain serious. BBC reporters near the strait describe conditions at the edge of an active battlefield.

Iran's internet shutdown has now broken records for wartime duration, deepening civilian frustration inside the country. Iranian missiles have struck a residential building in Haifa and an industrial zone in southern Israel, while Israel continues striking targets in Iran and Lebanon. OPEC+ has agreed to boost oil output but cautioned that recovery from infrastructure damage will be slow, with fuel price shocks already hitting workers as far away as Vietnam.

sources.title: Vietnam’s gig workers slammed by rising fuel costs amid fallout of Iran war
Trump declares victory after rescue but threats to US operation in Iran still loom
Trump issues expletive-laden threat to Iran over Hormuz Strait blockage
Iran war live: Tehran rejects Trump’s Tuesday deadline on Strait of Hormuz
'We are at the edge of a battlefield': BBC reports near Strait of Hormuz
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded
Trump threatens Iran in expletive-filled social media post
Frustration grows as Iran’s wartime internet shutdown breaks grim record
Video captures Iranian missile striking residential building in Haifa
OPEC+ agrees to hike oil output, warns of slow recovery after attacks
Iranian missile strikes industrial area in southern Israel

world.headline: Israel Strikes Lebanon and Gaza as Conflicts Grind On
world.body:
Israeli air strikes and shelling across Lebanon killed at least 14 people, adding to a rising death toll that has displaced tens of thousands. Residents describe lives turned upside down, with no clear end to the bombardment in sight.

In Gaza, an Israeli air strike hit a crowd in Gaza City as Hamas's armed wing flatly rejected disarmament demands, calling them unacceptable while Israel's offensive continues. Conditions in Gaza's tent camps are deteriorating sharply - rodent infestations are spreading and Christians marked Easter in mourning rather than celebration. The humanitarian crisis is drawing renewed criticism, with the Pope warning that the world is growing indifferent to violence.

sources.title: Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Has the humanitarian crisis in Gaza been ignored?
Hamas armed wing says disarmament demands not acceptable
Israeli air strike hits crowd in Gaza City
Hamas armed wing rejects disarmament while Israel continues attacks
Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Rodent infestations and attacks spread in Gaza tent camps
‘Refuse to celebrate’: Christians in Gaza mark sombre Easter amid genocide
Israeli air strikes, shelling across Lebanon add to rising death toll
At least 14 people killed in Israeli strikes across Lebanon
Pope warns world is becoming ‘indifferent’ to violence

world.headline: Explosives Found at Russian Gas Pipeline Spanning Serbia and Hungary
world.body:
Serbia and Hungary have both confirmed that explosives were discovered at a Russian gas pipeline running through their territories. Hungary, days before a national election, alleged a deliberate plot to blow up the infrastructure. No group has claimed responsibility.

The incident raises fresh concerns about the security of energy supply routes in Central Europe at a time when the broader region remains on edge. Investigators in both countries are working to establish the origin of the devices.

sources.title: Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Serbia, Hungary say explosives found at Russian gas pipeline

world.headline: Nigeria Rescues 31 Hostages; Ukraine and Syria Forge Security Pact
world.body:
Nigeria's army freed 31 hostages taken during an attack on a church, in one of the more significant rescue operations the military has carried out against armed groups this year. The government has not confirmed which group was responsible.

Separately, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced that Ukraine and Syria have agreed to cooperate on security and exchange military experience - a notable alignment between two countries that have each faced prolonged conflict. In the UK, seven protesters were arrested near an RAF base used by US forces, reflecting growing public opposition to British involvement in American military operations.

sources.title: Nigeria's army rescues 31 hostages after church attack
UK police arrest seven protesters near RAF base used by US
Ukraine and Syria to cooperate on security, Zelenskyy says

ai.headline: Microsoft Labels Copilot 'Entertainment Only' in Terms of Service
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service now classify Copilot as being "for entertainment purposes only," a quiet legal hedge that strips the product of any implied reliability for professional use. The language effectively disclaims responsibility if the AI gives bad advice, makes errors, or causes harm - a striking admission for a tool Microsoft has aggressively sold to enterprise customers.

The disclosure lands as AI companies broadly face pressure over accuracy and liability. Microsoft has invested billions positioning Copilot as a productivity cornerstone across Office, Windows, and developer tools. Burying "entertainment" language in the fine print suggests the company's legal team sees the product very differently than its marketing team does.

sources.title: Copilot is 'for entertainment purposes only', per Microsoft's terms of use
Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use

ai.headline: OpenAI Loses Ground as Investors Pivot to Anthropic
ai.body:
Investor sentiment is shifting away from OpenAI toward Anthropic, according to reporting that describes OpenAI's standing as a "fall from grace" among backers who once treated it as the unassailable leader in AI. The shift reflects growing concern about OpenAI's governance, product direction, and competition from rivals with cleaner corporate structures.

Meanwhile, developers keep building around both ecosystems. Open-source projects like GuppyLM aim to demystify how language models work, and tools like Nanocode push Claude-style coding workflows onto TPUs using JAX. The broader AI infrastructure is diversifying fast, which may be exactly why investors are hedging.

sources.title: OpenAI's fall from grace as investors race to Anthropic
Show HN: I built a tiny LLM to demystify how language models work
Nanocode: The best Claude Code that $200 can buy in pure JAX on TPUs

ai.headline: Japan's Labor Shortage Is a Live Test for Physical AI
ai.body:
Japan is deploying robots not to displace workers but to fill roles that go chronically unfilled - elder care, logistics, and manual labor that an aging population can no longer staff. The country's demographic crisis has turned it into an accidental proving ground for physical AI, and early results suggest the technology is more ready for real-world conditions than lab benchmarks implied.

The contrast with Western AI deployment debates is sharp. Where much of the conversation centers on job loss, Japan is treating robots as a staffing solution of last resort. That framing may spread as other countries face similar demographic pressures.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn't coming for your job; it's filling the one nobody wants
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

ai.headline: Employers Mine Personal Data to Suppress Your Salary
ai.body:
Companies are using personal data - spending habits, location history, financial signals - to estimate the lowest salary a candidate will accept before making an offer. The practice turns routine data collection into a negotiating weapon, systematically shifting leverage away from workers who have no visibility into what employers know or how it is used.

The tactic is legal in most jurisdictions and largely invisible to job seekers. It represents a concrete, immediate harm from data aggregation that sits well outside the usual AI safety discourse, but depends entirely on the same data pipelines and inference techniques driving the broader industry.

sources.title: Employers use your personal data to figure out the lowest salary you'll accept

btc.headline: Polymarket Pulls Iran Markets After Backlash, Odds Hit 63%
btc.body:
Prediction market Polymarket removed a set of Iran-related betting markets after users and observers pushed back hard against their framing. The controversy erupted shortly after Trump posted about Iran, sending odds of a US invasion this year to 63% on the platform.

The pulled markets had asked users to bet on scenarios involving rescuing Americans from Iran, a framing critics called exploitative and premature. Polymarket offered no detailed explanation for the removal. The episode highlights a recurring tension in prediction markets: where does useful forecasting end and harmful speculation begin.

sources.title: Polymarket odds of US invading Iran this year reach 63% after Trump's post
Polymarket pulls controversial Iran rescue markets after intense backlash

btc.headline: Bitcoin Fronts the Fed as ETF Money Rewires Market Behavior
btc.body:
Bitcoin is no longer waiting for the Federal Reserve to move. Analysts say it is now pricing in monetary policy shifts before they happen, a behavioral change driven by the flood of institutional capital entering through spot ETFs.

ETFs have pulled in a different class of investor - one that trades macro signals actively rather than reacting to crypto-native news. A Bitcoin Policy Institute executive added another layer, arguing Bitcoin and the US dollar share a symbiotic relationship rather than a competitive one, pushing back on the narrative that Bitcoin rises only when dollar confidence falls. Together, the arguments suggest Bitcoin is maturing into a genuine macro asset.

sources.title: Bitcoin and the US dollar have a 'symbiotic' relationship: BPI exec
Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause

btc.headline: Quantum Computing Threat to Bitcoin Is Real and Growing
btc.body:
A new explainer from CoinDesk lays out why quantum computing poses a genuine long-term threat to Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations. Current quantum machines cannot break Bitcoin's encryption, but the trajectory of the technology is the concern. Sufficiently powerful quantum computers could eventually derive private keys from public keys, putting older or exposed wallets at risk.

The Bitcoin developer community is aware of the problem, and post-quantum cryptography research is active. But upgrading Bitcoin's protocol requires broad consensus, which moves slowly. The gap between when the threat becomes real and when a fix is deployed is the risk no one can yet measure.

sources.title: A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin

btc.headline: Drift Incident Could Expose Protocol to Civil Negligence Claims
btc.body:
A crypto attorney says the recent Drift incident may meet the threshold for civil negligence, raising the prospect of legal liability for the decentralized exchange. The key question is whether Drift owed users a duty of care and whether a failure in that duty caused measurable harm.

The case is being watched closely because it could set a precedent for how negligence law applies to DeFi protocols. Decentralization has historically been used as a shield against legal accountability. If courts find that argument insufficient here, the implications for the broader DeFi sector would be significant.

sources.title: Crypto attorney says Drift incident may qualify as 'civil negligence'

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines roasted until silky and caramelised under a sweet white miso and mirin glaze
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before glazing — it lets the miso penetrate deeper and speeds up cooking by about five minutes

exercise.name: Monday Reset: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or heavy shopping bags, a clear corridor or garden path
exercise.description: Alternate between farmer carries, suitcase carries (one side only), and overhead carries for 40-metre lengths, resting 60 seconds between sets — brutally simple full-body conditioning that builds grip, core, and posture simultaneously

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity — Derek Parfit's take
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains the same object, but philosopher Derek Parfit extended this into a radical challenge to the very concept of personal identity, arguing in 'Reasons and Persons' that the self is not a persistent entity but a series of loosely connected psychological states
learn.why: Parfit's conclusion — that the boundaries of 'you' are vague and that this should actually be liberating rather than frightening — quietly dismantles assumptions behind guilt, ambition, and how you think about your future self, making it one of the most practically disorienting ideas in modern philosophy
